segments.s261-g9: چه is the interrogative 'what'. داری is the present of داشتن 'you have' used here to indicate a state/result (you are bearing/you have on). به روی اندر آورده is a verbal phrase 'to bring/put onto the face' (اندر = on/onto, آورده = past participle 'put/brought'); the clause asks what expression or thing the addressee has put upon his face. The final روی repeats the noun 'face' for emphasis/vocative effect.
segments.s261-g10: چه again introduces a question 'what'. اندیشه داری is a subject + verb: اندیشه 'a thought' + داری 'you have' = 'what thought do you have'. به دل ('in the heart') marks the location of that thought. در بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive construction asking the addressee to 'speak/declare it' (در 'in' here a enclitic preposition marking 'in' + بگوی = 'say' — the form given is the request to speak).
segments.s261-g11: گرت is an older contracted conditional 'if you' (from اگر + ت). رای جنگست is a nominal clause: رای 'plan/counsel' + جنگست ('is war' — جنگ + است fused) = 'your counsel is war/for war'. جنگ آزمای uses آزمای (imperative 'test/try') with جنگ as its object: 'try the war' i.e. 'test the battle' or 'prove yourself in battle'.
segments.s261-g12: ورت here functions as a contrasting/conditional particle ('but/if otherwise' — an older usage); رای برگشتن = 'the plan/counsel of turning back' (رای 'plan', برگشتن 'to return/retreat'). ایـدر 'here' marks the place. مپای is an archaic/poetic negative prohibition meaning roughly 'do not set foot' (don't step/move); together the clause warns 'if your counsel is to retreat, do not set foot here.'
segments.s261-g13: که here introduces an explanatory/result clause: 'that'. ننگست is a predicative expression 'it is shame/disgrace'. از این 'because of this/for this' gives the reason. بر تو = 'upon you' (the shame is directed at you). تو‌ای پهلوان is a vocative, addressing 'you, O hero'.
segments.s261-g14: بدین کار is the prepositional phrase 'at/for this deed' (بدین = 'by/with this', کار = 'deed/act'). خندند is past plural 'they laughed' or 'they will laugh (at)' — here it means people would laugh at the act. پیر و جوان is a coordinate phrase 'old and young' (i.e. everyone).
segments.s261-g15: همان لشکرست is a nominal identification: همان 'that very' + لشکرست ('is an army/host' — لشکر + است fused) = 'that is the same host'. این‌که begins the relative/explanatory clause 'that which/that who'. از ما بجنگ combines از ما 'against us' with بجنگ (infinitive/verb form 'to fight' or 'came to fight'), meaning 'that came to fight us.'
segments.s261-g16: برفتند is simple past plural 'they went/advanced'. و links to the following descriptive clause. رفته is a past participle 'gone' used resultatively. ز ('from') + روی ('face') + آب و رنگ ('water and color') is an idiomatic expression meaning their faces had lost moisture and color (they became pale/drained). The whole clause describes their appearance after going.
segments.s261-g81: شوم is the first‑person optative/subjunctive of شدن (‘may I become / let me’), a classical Persian way to introduce a resolved action. چرمه گامزن is a compact noun phrase (no ezafe shown in poetry) meaning ‘the striding one’s leather’ — i.e. the leather saddle or saddle‑cloth for the striding steed. زین کنم is a verb phrase where زین (saddle) is the object and کنم (present/subjunctive of کردن) functions as the first‑person auxiliary: literally ‘I make/saddle’ and here expresses the speaker’s intention to saddle the horse.
segments.s261-g82: سپیده دمان is a temporal adverbial meaning ‘at dawn’ or ‘daybreak’ (دمان here used metaphorically as the ‘edge/hem’ of dawn). جستن is the infinitive/verbal noun ‘to seek’ used with the finite auxiliary کنم that follows; کین is the object ‘vengeance’. The construction جستن کین کنم therefore reads as ‘I will go seek / I shall seek vengeance’, with the infinitive + کنم forming the speaker’s intention.
segments.s261-g65: The clause begins with the poetic preposition ز (z) 'from/of' introducing the source ('from the host/army'). فرستد is a past/action verb 'sent' (archaic/epic inflection -د). به پیشت is a prepositional phrase 'before you / in your presence' (به + پیشت), and بکین here is a proper name (vocative) 'Bikīn' — although the form looks like the verb بکن, context shows it names the dispatched man.
segments.s261-g66: Conditional clause introduced by اگر 'if'. برنوردی is a 2nd‑person form meaning 'you overcome/overrun' (an epic/poetic verbal form). بر او 'against/upon him' marks the target, and بر زمین 'onto the ground' gives the result/place; the repeated بر indicates direction/impact ('upon/over'), so the whole clause means 'if you overcome him and cast him to the ground.'
segments.s261-g67: ترا نام is the object 'your name' formed with the enclitic second‑person -ت plus the object marker را (here written separately as ت و را). از آن 'from that' gives the source, and برنیاید بلند is a negative verbal phrase: برنیاید (negated present/future sense 'will not arise/come about') plus بلند 'lofty/high' — together: 'your name will not rise high from that' (i.e. you will gain no renown from it).
segments.s261-g68: به ایرانیان 'to/for the Iranians' marks the affected group. نیز 'also/likewise' links this clause to the previous one, and ناید گزند is an impersonal negative expression 'harm does not come' (ناید here = 'does not come' + گزند 'harm'), meaning 'no harm will befall the Iranians.'
segments.s261-g69: وگر is a conditional 'but if'. بر تو 'upon you' marks the person affected. بر دست یابد uses the idiom یابیدن with بر دست (literally 'to attain the hand/upper hand') meaning 'to get hold of' or 'to seize'; یابد here = 'attain/achieve'. به خون 'in blood' indicates that the seizure results in bloodshed (i.e. you being wounded or bloodied).
segments.s261-g70: شوند is the plural present/subjunctive/epic form 'they become' (here narrative/future sense). این دلیران ترکان 'these brave Turks' is the subject phrase (این + دلیران + ترکان), and زبون is a predicate adjective meaning 'abject/overcome/lowly', so the clause says 'these brave Turks will be brought low/defeated.'
segments.s261-g71: نگه کرد هومان is a verb+subject order common in epic style: نگه کرد 'made/looked (a gaze)' and the proper name هومان follows as subject. به گفتار اوی is a prepositional phrase 'at his speech/words' (به + گفتار + اوی 'his'), so the whole means 'Homan fixed his gaze on his words.'
segments.s261-g72: همی here is a verbal/adverbial particle indicating continued or emphatic action ('staring/continuously'). خیره دانست is a two‑word predicate: خیره 'staring/astonished' and دانست 'he considered/deemed' (past), and پیکار اوی 'his battle/fighting' is the object; together: 'he (still) stared and considered his fighting' (i.e. he regarded his combat as striking/remarkable).
segments.s261-g33: و (and) begins the clause and connects this thought to the previous lines. دیگر (another/other) here means ‘another one’ or ‘others’ and introduces a further group. که is the relative/connector 'who/that' linking this phrase to the rest of the clause. از پهلوانان شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from/among the king's champions' with از marking the set and شاه (king) qualifying پهلوانان (champions/heroes).
segments.s261-g34: ندانم is the negative present first‑person form 'I do not know' (ن- prefix + دانم). چو here means 'like' (comparing). گودرز is the proper name Goudarz, the standard of comparison. کس را is 'anyone/any person' with را as the definite/object/topic marker emphasizing that person. به جاه is a short prepositional phrase 'in/with regard to rank/status' (به + جاه). The whole segment expresses 'I do not know anyone like Goudarz in rank.'
segments.s261-g35: به introduces qualities ('in' or 'for'); گردن‌فرازی is a noun meaning proud bearing/boastful bearing; و links the two qualities; مردانگی is the abstract noun 'manliness' or 'valor'. The phrase lists the personal qualities in which the referenced person excels.
segments.s261-g36: Another parallel phrase introduced by به ('in/for'): رای here means 'counsel/plan' rather than just 'opinion', هشیوار qualifies it as 'sound/sane/alert' counsel, و links to فرزانگی which names 'wisdom' or sagacity. The construction is a poetic list of virtues—'in prudent counsel and wisdom.'
segments.s261-g37: The numeral + دیگر ('three others') begins the phrase (the printed words omitted the numeral in the word list but the segment reads 'سه دیگر'). که is the relative 'who/that'. پرداغ is an adjective meaning 'burning/ablaze' or 'sore/grief-stricken' describing جگر (here metaphorical 'heart/seat of feeling'). دارد is the present 'has/possesses'. The clause means 'three others who have burning hearts.'
segments.s261-g38: پر از is the fixed combination 'full of'. از before خون functions like 'of/from' to link the fullness to blood (خون). دل here repeats the idea of 'heart' (literally 'blood of the heart' = deep sorrow). از درد uses از as 'from' and درد is emotional/physical 'pain' or 'sorrow'; چندان qualifies the extent 'so much/so great', and پسر ('son' or 'youth') names who these are. The phrase paints them as sons whose hearts are filled with the blood (i.e., grief) of such great suffering.
segments.s261-g39: که is the relative 'who/that'. از تن = 'from the body' (از + تن). سرانشان is 'their heads' (سران here means heads/leaders; the suffix -شان indicates 'their'). جدا is 'separate/apart'. مانده‌ایم is literally 'we remain / have been left' but here it functions idiomatically to indicate that the heads lie apart from the bodies (i.e., have been severed). The clause therefore describes that these sons' heads are separated from their bodies.
segments.s261-g40: زمین را uses را as the definite object marker ('the earth/ground' as object). به plus خون here indicates instrument/coverage 'with/in blood' (i.e., covered in blood). گرد in this older/poetic position conveys 'around/about' or intensifying adverbial sense, and بنشانده‌ایم is perfective first‑person plural 'we have set/placed' — together the idiom means 'we have made the earth sit in blood' i.e. 'we have drenched the ground with blood.'
segments.s261-g41: کنون ('now') sets the temporal frame. تا here has the sense 'so long as / while' introducing a temporal condition. The phrase به تنش اندرون جان بود is a classical word order: به + تنش + اندرون = 'within his body', جان 'life' is the real subject of بود (past 'was') — literally: 'while there was life within his body.'
segments.s261-g42: بر این کینه is a prepositional/locative phrase ('upon/with regard to this grudge'); چون مار پیچان بود is the predicate where چون ('like') introduces the simile and پیچان (a participial/adjectival form) means 'winding/coiling'; بود is the past copula — together: 'this grudge coiled like a snake.'
segments.s261-g43: چهارم is an ordinal ('fourth') introducing this numbered point; که is the conjunction introducing the subordinate clause ('that' / 'when'); لشکر میان دو کوه literally 'the army between two mountains' uses میان 'between' followed by the noun کوه 'mountain(s)'.
segments.s261-g44: فرود آوریدست is an archaic/perfect verbal complex meaning 'they have descended' or 'they alighted' (فرود + آوریدست); و کرده گروه is 'and (having) made/formed a troop' — کرده is a participial form 'having made' modifying گروه 'group/troop'. The overall sense is 'they descended and made/form(ed) a band.'
segments.s261-g45: ز هر سو که پویی is a conditional/generalizing clause: ز هر سو 'from every side', که introduces 'that/when/if', and پویی is a present/subjunctive 2nd-person form meaning 'you go' or 'you may go' (classical usage: 'whichever side you go'). بدو 'to him' is the indirect object and راه نیست 'there is no way/path' is the verbless existential construction with نیست.
segments.s261-g46: براندیش is an imperative or hortative meaning 'consider/think on'; the clause that follows is a verbless nominal clause: کین رنج کوتاه نیست literally 'the pain/suffering of this vengeance is not short' (کین 'vengeance/grudge' governs رنج 'suffering'); کوتاه نیست is the predicate 'is not short.'
segments.s261-g47: بکوشید باید is a doubled hortative/necessity: بکوشید (imperative/optative 'strive' or 2nd-pl present) and باید 'must/it is necessary'. The sequence ب + دان here is the poetic/archaic rendering of 'به آن' ('to/at that'), and تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that'); مگر is used classically to introduce a hopeful/speculative 'perhaps'.
segments.s261-g48: از آن کوه‌پایه 'from that foothill' uses از 'from' + demonstrative آن + compound noun کوه‌پایه (foothill). برآرند is a 3rd-person plural subjunctive ('may they bring up/raise') governed by the purpose clause introduced by تا/مگر; سر 'head' is the object placed after the verb (poetic inversion).
segments.s261-g49: مگر introduces an excluding conditional here (= "unless"). مانده گردند is a periphrastic present-subjunctive/optative sense (‘remain / be left’) with گردند as the subjunctive verb; سستی is the noun object ("laxity, weakness") placed before the verb کنند, where کنند is the present subjunctive/optative third-person plural meaning “they show / act with.” The whole clause means “unless they remain and show weakness.”
segments.s261-g50: به جنگ اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning “into/against the inner battle” (به + noun + اندرون). پیشدستی کردن is a compound meaning “to take the initiative / act first,” and کنند is again the subjunctive/optative third-person plural “they do/act.” The word order places the directional phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s261-g51: چو is a temporal conjunction here (= "when"). از کوه بیرون expresses motion out of the mountain (from the mountain outwards). کند is third-person present/subjunctive (poetic use) governing لشکرش, so the clause means “when his army/goes out of the mountain.” The direct object/agent (لشکرش) follows the verb in this inverted poetic word order.
segments.s261-g52: یکی here is an adverbial/quantifying use ("all at once / one [sudden]") modifying تیرباران (a barrage/shower of arrows). کنم is first-person present/subjunctive used for a future/optative “I will do / I will cause.” بر سرش is the prepositional complement “upon his/their head,” where -ش is the possessive suffix referring back to لشکرش.
segments.s261-g53: چو introduces a simile/temporal clause (“when / as”). دیوار گرد (“a wall around”) uses گرد after دیوار to mean “around/about,” and اندر is a locative particle “in/within.” آریمشان is first‑person plural present ('we arrange/array them'), with the enclitic -شان “them.” The clause reads “when we set them as a wall around (them).”
segments.s261-g54: This is a parallel simile introduced again by چو. شیر ژیان is a compound image (literally “lion + life”), here functioning as “a living/fierce lion.” در بر آریمشان uses در بر + verb to mean “we place/bring them in front / surround them.” The repeated آریمشان keeps the same first‑person plural sense.
segments.s261-g55: بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase “against/upon them.” بگردد is a third‑person optative/subjunctive verb here meaning “may (it) turn / be directed,” and همه کام ما is the nominal phrase “all our desire(s)” which functions as the grammatical subject of the wish. The clause thus prays that all their desires be focused on those enemies.
segments.s261-g56: برآید is an optative/potential verb meaning “may (it) arise / come forth.” به خورشید marks direction/height (“to the sun”), and بر نام ما places the theme “our name/fame” as that which should rise. The phrase literally reads “may it come up to the sun on our name,” i.e. “may our name rise to the sun (become renowned).”
segments.s261-g1: This is a short noun phrase introduced by an archaic/conjunctive preposition-clitic (و ز → “and/from”) meaning “and from.” The head is لشکر ترک (“Turk(ish) host/army”); هومان is the proper name in apposition and دلیر is a postpositive epithet (“the brave”), a typical classical Persian order: [and-from] + [that army] + [name] + [epithet].
segments.s261-g2: به پیش برادر بیامد is a motion/position clause: به پیش = “towards/in the presence of,” followed by the addressee/target برادر. بیامد is simple past 3sg “came/advanced.” The simile چو شیر (“like a lion”) follows the verb to modify the manner of coming. Word order (prepositional phrase → target → verb → simile) is a normal, somewhat elevated word order in classical Persian.
segments.s261-g3: که‌ای here is a vocative/attention particle (= «که ای» ≈ “O!”) addressing the hero (پهلوان). رد افراسیاب is a genitive phrase where رد means “passage/retreat/rout” (the movement associated with Afrasiab) and افراسیاب names the agent (the rout/flight of Afrasiab).
segments.s261-g4: گرفت is simple past “seized/took.” اندر این دشت gives the location (“in this plain”), and ما را شتاب is an accusative construction meaning “haste seized us” (ما را = “us” with the object marker را, شتاب = “haste/panic” as the thing that seized them). The verb precedes locative and object here, a common classical order for emphasis.
segments.s261-g5: به هفتم فراز is an adverbial time/turn phrase: به + ordinal هفتم + فراز (“rise/turn/summit”), literally “at the seventh rise/turn.” آمد is past “came,” and این روزگار (“this fate/time”) is the subject placed after the verb for poetic emphasis; روزگار here is best rendered “fate” or “turn of fortune.”
segments.s261-g6: میان بسته is a participial phrase: میان = “the middle/midst” and بسته (past participle of بستن) = “closed/locked,” together meaning “locked/engaged in the middle.” در جنگ is the locative “in battle,” and چندین سوار = “several horsemen.” The phrase describes that in the middle of the fight several horsemen were interlocked/engaged tightly.
segments.s261-g7: از آهن is an instrumental/descriptive prepositional phrase, “with/through iron” (poetic shorthand for weapons/steel). میان سوده is a compact poetic phrase modifying the scene (literally “the middle [smeared/struck/engaged]” — here describing close combat), and و دل ز کین = “and hearts from/because of hatred” (ز = poetic “from/because of,” کین = “hatred/vengeance”). Taken together it pictures weapons clashing and hearts filled with hatred. Note: سوده is an archaic/poetic form whose exact lexical sense does not match the provided senses here.
segments.s261-g8: نهاده is a past participle/verb meaning “placed/set” (perfective), taking دو دیده (“two eyes”) as its object; به ایران‌زمین is the prepositional phrase “upon/to the land of Iran.” The construction means “(they) set/fixed two eyes on the Iranian land,” i.e. they directed their gaze/ambition toward Iran.
segments.s261-g73: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase meaning "gave such an answer/answered thus" (چنین = thus; داد = gave; پاسخ = answer). کز introduces a relative/subordinate clause ("that/which/who"); ایران سوار literally 'rider of Iran' (Iran as possessor) and it stays syntactically tied to the predicate in the next segment, so this segment ends with the noun phrase awaiting the verb.
segments.s261-g74: نباشد here negates existence/occurrence (classical negative copula: 'there is not / will not be'); که is the complementizer introducing the content of that negation ('that/who'); با من کند کارزار is the clause specifying what does not occur: با + pronoun = 'against/with me' (poetic use meaning 'fight me'), کند is present/subjunctive form 'would do/make', and کارزار = 'battle/war'.
segments.s261-g75: ترا (ت + را) is the topicalized/accusative form 'you' (here marking the person whose quality is described). خود همین = 'this very' (خود emphatic), مهربانیست is a nominal verbless construction 'is kindness/this is kindness' and خوی = 'disposition/nature' serves as the predicate; overall: 'This very kindness is (your) disposition.'
segments.s261-g76: آرزوی کارزار = 'a desire/wish for battle' (آرزوی governs the genitive noun کارزار). آمدست is the perfective/present perfect poetic form 'has come' (i.e. 'has arisen'), and مرا (م + را) functions as the dative/experiencer 'to me' (word order is inverted for emphasis: literally 'to me the desire for battle has come').
segments.s261-g77: وگر = 'if/but if' introducing the condition. کت به کین جستن is a purposive infinitive phrase: کت ('coat/armour') + به + جستن ('to seek') with کین 'vengeance' as the object of جستن — together meaning 'to put on the coat to seek vengeance' or 'to take up the coat in order to seek revenge'. آهنگ نیست = 'there is no intention/aim' (آهنگ = intention/aim; نیست negates it).
segments.s261-g78: به دلت اندرون = 'into your heart' with به + دل + enclitic ت (your) and اندرون 'within' (poetic word order). آتش جنگ = 'fire of war' (metaphorical 'flame/passion of warfare'), and نیست negates its presence: 'there is no war-fire inside your heart.'
segments.s261-g79: کنم is first-person 'I do / I will do' (poetic present/future); آنچ = 'that which' / 'what'; باید = modal 'must' (it is necessary); بدین رزمگاه is 'in this battlefield' (بدین = ب + این 'in/to this' — poetic compound — and رزمگاه = battlefield). The phrase means 'I will do what must be done on this battlefield.'
segments.s261-g80: نمایم is first-person 'I will show / I shall display'; هنرها (plural) = 'skills/arts/feats'; به indicates direction/beneficiary 'to/for'; ایران سپاه = 'the army of Iran' (poetic word order with the modifier after به). The clause states 'I will display my skills to the Iranian army.'
segments.s261-g57: This is a nominal clause with the copula omitted (typical in Persian poetry). تو is the subject ('you'); پشت سپاهی is a noun phrase 'the rear/behind of the army' (پشت = rear/behind; سپاهی here adjectival/noun phrase 'of the army'); سالار شاه is a second predicate 'the king's commander' (سالار = commander; شاه = king). The two predicates are joined by و.
segments.s261-g58: This phrase describes a bestowed object: برآورده is a past-participle/adjectival form 'raised/produced/provided' that qualifies the final noun کلاه ('helmet/hat'); از introduces the source ('from'); چرخ گردان is a compound where چرخ refers to the turning wheel of fate/heaven and گردان is its adjectival sense 'revolving'. Word order is typical poetic inversion: participle + source + modifier + head noun.
segments.s261-g59: This is an elliptical relative clause headed by کسی کو 'anyone who' (کسی = someone/anyone). کو here is an archaic relative/particle linking to the following predicate. به نام بلندش نیاز is a prepositional phrase where به marks the object of نیاز ('need of his lofty name') and بلندش = بلند + enclitic ش 'his' ('his lofty [name]'). The verb ('نباشد') is deferred to the next segment.
segments.s261-g60: نباشد is the negative of 'to be' used here in a jussive/optative sense ('let there not be' / 'there is not'). چه گردد introduces a rhetorical/result clause ('what would become / what would happen'); همی is an emphatic/adverb meaning 'indeed' or 'still'. گرد (here functioning as the noun 'wheel') + آز ('greed') forms 'the wheel of greed' that is affected by the verb گردد ('would turn / would become'); poetic word order separates the verb and its noun phrase.
segments.s261-g61: This is an introducing noun phrase: و دیگر 'and furthermore/again' + که 'that/which' heads a relative clause. از نامداران جنگ literally 'from the renowned (men) of war' (از = from/among; نامداران = the renowned; جنگ qualifies them: 'renowned warriors of battle'). This phrase specifies the class of people referred to.
segments.s261-g62: نیاید is a negative present/jussive form meaning 'may/must not come' or 'does not come' depending on context; here it functions as the predicate 'let no ... come'. کسی is the indefinite 'anyone'; نزد ما is a locative phrase 'to our presence / before us' (نزد = in the presence of); بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'without delay / immediately' placed at the end to qualify the coming.
segments.s261-g63: ز گردان 'from among the ranks/troop' (ز = from; گردان = troop) starts the phrase. کسی را که is an object-fronting relative construction ('someone whom/that'); را is the object marker attached to کسی. بی‌نام‌تر is a comparative adjective 'more nameless / less renowned' modifying that کسی; the comparative relation continues into the next segment.
segments.s261-g64: ز introduces the comparison 'than' (poetic use); جنگ سواران 'the horsemen/warriors of war' is the standard of comparison (جنگ = war; سواران = riders/horsemen). بی‌آرام‌تر is the comparative 'more restless' (بی- 'without' + آرام 'calm' + -تر comparative) completing the comparison begun in the previous segment.
segments.s261-g25: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 3rd-person singular ('heard') placed before its subject (poetic inversion). پیران is the (plural) subject 'the elders'. ز + name + سخن forms a short genitive/source phrase 'the speech/words from Houman' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from').
segments.s261-g26: بدو ('to him') is an adpositional clitic phrase marking the indirect object. گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' (classical diction; the lexical senses listed are conversational but this token is verbal). مشتاب is an imperative 'hurry!' (positive command). و links the two imperatives. تندی مکن is a negative command made of the noun تندی ('fierceness/harshness') plus the prohibitive مکن ('do not'), meaning 'do not be fierce/harsh'.
segments.s261-g27: بدان‌ای is an imperative 'know this' with the vocative ای attached ('O') and برادر is the vocative 'brother' (form of address). که begins a subordinate/relative clause 'that/which'. این points to 'this', and رزمخواه is a compound noun 'one who seeks battle' (an aspiring fighter); the whole segment is an introductory clause: 'Know, brother, that this warrior...'.
segments.s261-g28: که repeats the relative marker 'who/that'. آمد is past 'came'. چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. پیش ما is a locative phrase 'in our presence / before us' (پیش here = 'in the presence of'). با سپاه is an instrumental/with-phrase 'with an army'. Word order is typical of classical Persian, with verb before some modifiers and fairly compact phrase links.
segments.s261-g29: گزین here functions as a noun 'the chosen/selected (one)'. بزرگان is a genitive/descriptor 'of the great ones' (elders/major figures). کیخسروست is a nominal predicate with the proper name + copula suffix '-ست' meaning 'is Kay Khosrow's / belongs to Kay Khosrow' (classical possessive/predicative linkage).
segments.s261-g30: سر is used figuratively as 'chief/head'. نامداران is a plural attributive 'the famed/renowned ones'. هر here means 'every/each' and attaches to پهلوست, a predicative compound meaning 'is at the side/flank' or 'occupies the flank/position' — poetically: 'he is chief at every flank' (i.e. preeminent on all sides). The segment uses compact, elevated diction common in epic Persian.
segments.s261-g31: یکی آنک is a classical formula 'one (is that) that...' introducing an example. یکی here = 'a certain / one', آنک = relative 'that/which/who'. کیخسرو is the proper name (subject of the following verb), and از شاه من is a short apposition 'of my king' (classical use of از with a title to mark relationship/appellation: 'Kay Khosrow, my king'). The phrase establishes Kay Khosrow as the agent who will honour the man mentioned.
segments.s261-g32: بدو is the clitic prepositional phrase 'to him' (indirect object). سر here is literal/figurative 'head' (the action is 'raise the head' as a mark of honour). فرازد is a past/characteristic form 'he exalted/raised' (3rd-person). به هر انجمن is a locative phrase 'in/at every assembly' (به = in/at; هر = every; انجمن = assembly). The clause means Kay Khosrow honors him in every council.
segments.s261-g17: کز (an archaic contraction of 'که از') introduces an agent or source: 'from them / by them'. The clause 'همه رزمگه کشته بود' is a past-state description: 'همه' = 'everyone/all', 'رزمگه' = 'the battlefield' (here the people on it), 'کشته' is a past participle 'killed/slain', and 'بود' is the past copula giving the past perfective sense 'had been / were'. Word order is topic ('کز ایشان') + comment ('همه رزمگه کشته بود').
segments.s261-g18: This is a single predicate describing the state of the ground. 'زمین' = 'the earth/ground', 'سر به سر' is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'throughout/everywhere' (literally 'head to head'), 'رود خون' = 'a river of blood' (noun phrase, word order reversed for poetic effect), 'گشته بود' = 'had become' (perfective/resultant state using participle + past 'بود').
segments.s261-g19: The negative frame 'نه ... کم است' means 'it is not few' → 'there is no lack'. 'زین' here is an older/contractional prepositional element meaning 'of/from these' (classical usage), 'نامداران' = 'the renowned', 'سواری' = 'horsemen/riders', and 'کم است' = 'is few / lacking' negated by 'نه' to yield 'is not few / there is no shortage'. Word order is emphatic: negative particle + genitive phrase + predicate.
segments.s261-g20: Parallel negative clause introduced by 'نه' (‘nor’). 'آن دوده را' is topicalized with the object marker 'را' (literally 'that smoke' as the topic), and the verb 'است' copulates it with 'پهلوان رستم' — 'پهلوان رستم است' = 'Rostam is the champion (of it)'. The word order places the topical object first for emphasis.
segments.s261-g21: 'گرت' is a classical conditional particle meaning 'if' (contraction of 'اگر تو'). 'آرزو نیست' = 'there is no desire' (negative copula), and the infinitive noun phrase 'خون ریختن' = 'to spill/shed blood' functions as the object of 'آرزو'. The sequence says 'if you do not desire to shed blood...'.
segments.s261-g22: 'نخواهی' here is second-person singular present/future of خواستن used in the sense 'you will not/will not wish' (in context: 'you will not [do something]'); 'همی' is an emphatic/classical enclitic adverb ('indeed/also/just'), and 'لشکر انگیختن' is an infinitival construction 'to raise an army'. Together it means 'you will not (indeed) cause/raise an army.'
segments.s261-g23: 'ز' (poetic 'از') = 'from', marking the source: 'from the warriors'. 'جنگ‌آوران' = 'warriors' (source group), 'لشکری' = 'troops/soldiery' (the thing to be chosen), and 'برگزین' is the imperative 'choose/select!'. Word order is source ('from the warriors') + object ('an army/troops') + imperative verb.
segments.s261-g24: This is a sequence of imperatives/commands. 'به من ده' = 'give (them) to me' (به = 'to', من = 'me', ده = imperative 'give' — note: the item 'ده' isn't listed among the provided words but is grammatically the imperative of 'دادن'). 'تو بنگر' is a direct address 'you, look/behold' (تو explicitly stated for emphasis) and 'کنون رزم و کین' = 'now, (at) battle and vengeance' — 'کنون' adverb 'now' modifies the imperative 'بنگر', and 'رزم و کین' is the content to attend to. The two clauses are coordinated instructions: hand the troops over and you attend to the fight and revenge.

sentences.s261-s5: What have you put on your face? What thought do you hold in your heart? Tell it.
sentences.s261-s6: If your plan is for war, try the battle; but if your plan is to turn back, do not set foot here.
sentences.s261-s7: For that would be a disgrace for you, O hero; old and young would laugh at such a deed.
sentences.s261-s8: That is the very host that came out to fight us; they went forward, and their faces had lost water and color.
sentences.s261-s41: Let me saddle the striding steed with leather; at daybreak I will seek vengeance.
sentences.s261-s33: He sent Bikīn with troops ahead of you; if you can overthrow him onto the ground.
sentences.s261-s34: That will bring you no fame, and it will not do the Iranians any harm either.
sentences.s261-s35: But if he gets hold of you and you are bloodied, these brave Turks will be brought low.
sentences.s261-s36: Homan fixed his gaze on his words and, staring, regarded his fighting.
sentences.s261-s17: And another — among the king's champions — I know no one like Goudarz in rank.
sentences.s261-s18: In proud bearing and in manly valor; in wise counsel and in sagacity (he stands out).
sentences.s261-s19: Three others whose hearts are aflame — their hearts full of blood and heartache from such great suffering, (they are) sons.
sentences.s261-s20: — whose heads have been severed from their bodies; we have drenched the ground with blood.
sentences.s261-s21: Now, so long as there was life within his body, this hatred coiled like a snake.
sentences.s261-s22: Fourth, the army had descended between two mountains and formed a band.
sentences.s261-s23: From every side that you go toward him there is no way; consider that the hardship of this grudge is not short.
sentences.s261-s24: Strive — you must — at it, so that perhaps from that foothill they may raise a head.
sentences.s261-s25: Unless they remain and grow lax and take the initiative in the inner fighting.
sentences.s261-s26: When his host comes out of the mountain, I will shower arrows on their heads at once.
sentences.s261-s27: When we array them around like a wall, when we surround them like a living lion.
sentences.s261-s28: May all our desires be directed against them; may our name rise up to the sun.
sentences.s261-s1: And from that Turkish host Homan the brave came forward before his brother like a lion.
sentences.s261-s2: O champion! Afrasiab’s rout swept through — in this plain haste seized us.
sentences.s261-s3: At the seventh turning this fate came — in the midst of the fighting several horsemen were locked together.
sentences.s261-s4: With iron between them and hearts full of hatred, two eyes were fixed upon the land of Iran.
sentences.s261-s37: He answered thus: that there is no rider of Iran who would make war against me.
sentences.s261-s38: This very kindness is your nature; a desire for battle has come upon me.
sentences.s261-s39: And if it is not your intention to seek vengeance by donning the coat, there is no fire of war within your heart.
sentences.s261-s40: I will do what must be done in this battlefield; I will show the skills to the army of Iran.
sentences.s261-s29: You are the rear of the army and the king's commander; a helmet raised by the revolving wheel of fate.
sentences.s261-s30: Let there be no one who lacks need of his lofty name; otherwise what would happen — the wheel of greed would indeed turn.
sentences.s261-s31: And furthermore, let no one of the renowned warriors of war come into our presence without delay.
sentences.s261-s32: From the ranks, whoever is more nameless and more restless than the horsemen of war.
sentences.s261-s13: When the elders heard Houman's words, they said to him, "Hasten, and do not be violent."
sentences.s261-s14: Know this, O brother: this fighting-man who has come before us with an army.
sentences.s261-s15: He is the chosen of the great ones, belonging to Kay Khosrow; the chief among the famed, at every flank.
sentences.s261-s16: One (such man) is he whom Kay Khosrow — my king — raises his head to in every assembly.
sentences.s261-s9: They had slain everyone on the battlefield; the ground throughout had turned into a river of blood.
sentences.s261-s10: There is no shortage of horsemen among these renowned men, nor is Rostam the champion of that smoke.
sentences.s261-s11: If you have no desire to shed blood, you will not raise an army either.
sentences.s261-s12: Select troops from the warriors; give them to me, and you look now to battle and vengeance.
